preview_text:
The model forecasts Spain as a heavy favorite with an 90% win probability, leaving Saudi Arabia with virtually no chance of victory in the model's frame. The sportsbook consensus aligns closely, placing Spain at 86%, a gap of only 4 percentage points and well within normal variance across books.

The expected match shape is heavily tilted toward a Spain victory. Draws are assigned low probability by both sources—around 10% or less—reflecting the wide quality differential. A Saudi Arabia win registers as negligible under the model but receives modest credence from sportsbooks at 4%, suggesting they assign some nonzero tail risk that the model's bias correction does not. The contest is unlikely to be competitive in statistical expectation.

Model and market are substantially aligned on the headline outcome. Spain's win probability ranges between 86% and 90% across the three sources, and draw odds cluster tightly between 9% and 11%. The sportsbook assigns Saudi Arabia 4%, while the model rounds to zero; this small divergence reflects differing treatment of extreme underdog scenarios. Overall, no material disagreement exists on match direction.
